summary: Fowler addresses the common objection that certain specialties (security, UI design, databases) cannot be done incrementally and must be planned upfront. He argues that while he may lack expertise in specific fields, history has repeatedly proven such claims wrong. The key challenge is that incremental design requires 'enabling practices' to prevent chaos—testing, continuous integration, and refactoring for software; data migration for databases. Without these practices, incremental approaches will fail, but the benefits of incrementalism—especially risk management and handling volatile requirements—make it worth experimenting to find the right enabling practices for each domain.
key_points: Claims that 'you can't do X incrementally' have repeatedly been proven wrong across domains like application design and database design
Incremental design without enabling practices leads to messy, entropic designs—you need mechanisms that make designs converge toward order
Each specialty requires discovering its own enabling practices, which may differ from software's testing/CI/refactoring trio
Phased upfront design fails frequently and handles volatile requirements poorly
Risk management is the primary argument for incrementalism—it reduces vulnerability to late-stage surprises and schedule slippage
money_quotes: I've seen enough cases where people have said 'you can't use incremental design for x' only to find you can.
To make incremental design work you need something that makes the design converge into order.
Until you find a good set of enabling practices, incremental design is thin ice.
Phased up-front design approaches fail far too frequently—furthermore they do a poor job in the chance of volatile requirements.
Without trying out your designs, you are too vulnerable to things not working out the way you think they would.
key_insight: Incremental design can likely work in any domain, but only if you discover the right enabling practices that prevent entropy and make the design converge toward order.
